content.text: Azure Arc-enabled servers support two orthogonal governance mechanisms: VM extensions (imperative) and Azure Policy with Guest Configuration (declarative). Extensions install software agents on the server — Azure Monitor Agent, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Custom Script, Key Vault certificate sync — triggered by ARM resource creation or Azure Policy DINE effect. Guest Configuration policies audit OS-level settings — file permissions, registry values, installed software, running services — using DSC, Chef InSpec, or custom scripts packaged as GC packages. The two often work together: a DINE policy deploys an extension, and a separate GC policy audits that the extension is configured correctly.

attrs.alt: Arc VM Extensions (imperative via gc_extension) vs Guest Configuration Policy (declarative via gc_config)

content.text: Key distinction: extensions are ARM resources that install software. Guest Configuration policies are Azure Policy assignments that audit configuration. A DINE effect policy bridges the two: it creates the extension ARM resource when one is missing. Extensions are installed once (with update lifecycle); GC policies poll on a schedule (default: every 15 minutes).
Ships telemetry to Log Analytics via DCR
Runs arbitrary script once at install
Syncs Key Vault certificates to local store

Guest Configuration policies evaluate OS settings on Arc servers using a local agent (the GuestConfigurationForLinux or Windows extension). The gc_config service downloads a policy package (.zip containing a DSC MOF or InSpec profile), runs it locally, and reports compliance state back to Azure Policy. Built-in GC policies cover CIS Benchmark Levels 1 and 2, NIST SP 800-53, Windows Security Baseline, and Linux Security Baseline. For Audit-effect policies, no managed identity is needed. For DINE-effect policies (which deploy extensions or configurations), the policy assignment requires a SystemAssigned managed identity with the Guest Configuration Resource Contributor role.

# Guest Configuration policy: audit OS settings on Arc servers
# GC policies use Audit or DeployIfNotExists effect.
# Audit: checks settings, reports compliance — no changes made.
# DINE: deploys the GC extension and managed identity if missing, then audits.

# Assign a built-in GC policy — Windows security baseline audit
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'arc-win-security-baseline' \
  --display-name 'Windows security baseline audit on Arc servers' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/72650e9f-97bc-4b2a-ab5f-9781a9fcecbc' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-servers-rg' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

# For DINE-effect policies, grant the assignment's MSI the required role:
ASSIGNMENT_MSI=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'arc-win-security-baseline' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-servers-rg' \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Guest Configuration Resource Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id "$ASSIGNMENT_MSI" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-servers-rg'

# Trigger a compliance scan for existing resources (initial evaluation takes up to 24h otherwise)
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'arc-gc-baseline-remediation' \
  --policy-assignment 'arc-win-security-baseline' \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant

# Check compliance state per resource
az policy state summarize \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --policy-assignment 'arc-win-security-baseline' \
  --query 'results.nonCompliantResources' -o tsv

# Detailed non-compliant resource list via Resource Graph
az graph query -q \
  "PolicyResources
   | where type == 'microsoft.policyinsights/policystates'
   | where resourceType =~ 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines'
   | where complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
   | project ResourceName=name, PolicyName=policyDefinitionName,
             ResourceGroup=resourceGroup, Timestamp=timestamp" \
  --query 'data' -o table
DINE Policy: Auto-Deploy Azure Monitor Agent

The most common DINE policy pattern for Arc servers deploys Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) to all Arc servers that are missing it. Two separate DINE policies are needed: one to install the AMA extension, and one to create the Data Collection Rule (DCR) association — AMA without a DCR association sends no data to Log Analytics. Assign and remediate them in order: AMA first, then DCR. Each policy assignment requires its own SystemAssigned MSI with Contributor permissions — failing to grant the role is the most common reason DINE remediation tasks fail silently.

# DINE policy: automatically deploy Azure Monitor Agent to all Arc servers
# Effect: DeployIfNotExists — deploys the extension if it is missing.
# Built-in policy ID for AMA on Arc (Linux): b0cdcf83-5f9a-4e26-8e7f-2a8e47f12a10

# 1. Assign the DINE policy
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'arc-deploy-ama' \
  --display-name 'Deploy AMA to Arc Linux servers' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/b0cdcf83-5f9a-4e26-8e7f-2a8e47f12a10' \
  --params '{
    "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId":{"value":"/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws-name>"}
  }' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

# 2. Grant the MSI the Contributor role at subscription scope
#    AMA extension deployment requires Contributor on the Arc server resources
DINE_MSI=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'arc-deploy-ama' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>' \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id "$DINE_MSI" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>'

# 3. Create a remediation task for servers missing AMA
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'arc-ama-remediation' \
  --policy-assignment 'arc-deploy-ama' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant

# 4. Monitor remediation progress
az policy remediation show \
  --name 'arc-ama-remediation' \
  --query '{Status:provisioningState, Succeeded:deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments, Failed:deploymentSummary.failedDeployments}' \
  -o json

# 5. DINE policy for DCR association (second policy needed after AMA is installed)
#    Each extension install and DCR association is a separate DINE policy.
#    Built-in: 'Configure Linux Arc-enabled machines to run Azure Monitor Agent'
#    Built-in: 'Configure Linux Arc-enabled machines with data collection rule association'
#    Assign both and remediate in order: AMA first, then DCR association.
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'arc-dcr-association' \
  --display-name 'Associate Arc Linux servers with DCR' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/2ea82cdd-f2e8-4500-af75-67a2e084ca74' \
  --params '{
    "dcrResourceId":{"value":"/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Insights/dataCollectionRules/<dcr-name>"}
  }' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

When built-in GC policies don't cover a specific compliance requirement, author a custom GC package using the GuestConfiguration PowerShell module. The package is a DSC configuration (PowerShell DSC or InSpec) compiled to a .mof file, zipped, uploaded to Azure Storage, and published as a custom Azure Policy definition. The policy definition references the package URI and SHA-256 hash — the hash must be updated whenever the package changes. Custom GC packages support both Audit (check-only) and DeployIfNotExists (check-and-fix) modes on Linux and Windows Arc servers.

# Custom Guest Configuration policy: author a new compliance rule
# Use case: audit that a specific file exists and has correct permissions
# Tool: GuestConfiguration PowerShell module (cross-platform)

# 1. Install the GuestConfiguration module
Install-Module -Name GuestConfiguration -Force
Install-Module -Name PSDscResources -Force

# 2. Author a DSC configuration (PowerShell)
cat << 'EOF' > AuditNginxConfig.ps1
Configuration AuditNginxConfig {
    Import-DscResource -ModuleName PSDscResources

    Node 'localhost' {
        File NginxConfExists {
            Type = 'File'
            DestinationPath = '/etc/nginx/nginx.conf'
            Ensure = 'Present'
        }
    }
}
AuditNginxConfig -OutputPath ./DSCOutput
EOF
pwsh AuditNginxConfig.ps1

# 3. Package the DSC config as a Guest Configuration package
New-GuestConfigurationPackage \
  -Name 'AuditNginxConfig' \
  -Configuration './DSCOutput/localhost.mof' \
  -Type Audit \
  -Path './GCPackages'

# 4. Publish the package to Azure Storage
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name '<storage-account>' \
  --container-name 'gc-packages' \
  --file './GCPackages/AuditNginxConfig.zip' \
  --name 'AuditNginxConfig.zip' \
  --auth-mode login

PACKAGE_URI=$(az storage blob url \
  --account-name '<storage-account>' \
  --container-name 'gc-packages' \
  --name 'AuditNginxConfig.zip' \
  --auth-mode login -o tsv)

PACKAGE_HASH=$(sha256sum ./GCPackages/AuditNginxConfig.zip | awk '{print $1}')

# 5. Create a custom policy definition from the package
New-GuestConfigurationPolicy \
  -ContentUri "$PACKAGE_URI" \
  -DisplayName 'Audit nginx.conf exists on Arc Linux servers' \
  -Description 'Checks that /etc/nginx/nginx.conf exists on Arc servers' \
  -Path './PolicyDefinitions' \
  -Version 1.0.0 \
  -Platform Linux

# 6. Publish the policy definition to Azure
New-AzPolicyDefinition \
  -Name 'AuditNginxConfig' \
  -Policy './PolicyDefinitions/AuditNginxConfig/AuditIfNotExists.json'

# 7. Assign the custom GC policy (Audit effect, no DINE needed for Audit-only)
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'audit-nginx-conf' \
  --display-name 'Audit nginx.conf on Arc Linux servers' \
  --policy 'AuditNginxConfig' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-servers-rg'
Assigning DINE GC policy without granting MSI the Contributor role
Remediation task fails silently; extension never installs
Grant Guest Configuration Resource Contributor (for GC) or Contributor (for extensions) to the assignment MSI
Assigning AMA policy without a DCR association policy
AMA installs but sends no data — workspace shows no Arc data
Assign both 'deploy AMA' and 'associate DCR' policies and remediate in order
Using Audit-effect policy expecting it to fix non-compliant resources
Policy reports non-compliant but makes no changes
Use DINE effect with a remediation task for auto-fix; Audit is report-only
Guest Configuration policies show Unknown on all Arc servers
Authoring custom GC package without versioning
Policy definition and package hash mismatch causes deployment failures
Increment Version in New-GuestConfigurationPolicy whenever the package changes; old assignments must be re-targeted
Expecting instant compliance after policy assignment
Compliance evaluation takes up to 24h without remediation
Create a remediation task immediately after assignment for ExistingNonCompliant resources

Rather than assigning four separate policies to enable AMA, DCR, Defender, and GC audit on Arc servers, bundle them into a single policy set definition (initiative). One initiative assignment creates a single managed identity and one compliance report in Azure Portal instead of four. The initiative's MSI must be granted all roles required by all DINE policies in the set — Contributor for extension deployments, Guest Configuration Resource Contributor for GC packages, and Log Analytics Contributor for workspace operations. After assignment, create individual remediation tasks per DINE policy reference ID within the initiative — remediation tasks target one policy definition at a time, not the full initiative.

Beyond deploying extensions via DINE, Azure Policy can enforce extension governance: Deny policies block unauthorized extensions (CustomScript is a common target in security-sensitive environments — it allows arbitrary code execution via ARM); Audit policies flag outdated extension versions that need upgrading; Resource Graph queries give real-time extension inventory. Enable automatic upgrade (enableAutomaticUpgrade: true) on extensions that publish frequent security patches — AMA, MDE, and GuestConfiguration all benefit from auto-upgrade within their minor version range. Auto-upgrade does not apply to major versions or extensions marked as manual.

# Extension policy patterns: enforce, block, and audit extensions fleet-wide

# Pattern 1: DENY unwanted extensions (security-sensitive environments)
# Block CustomScript extension — prevents arbitrary code execution via ARM
cat << 'EOF' > deny_custom_script.json
{
  "mode": "All",
  "policyRule": {
    "if": {
      "allOf": [
        {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/extensions"},
        {"field": "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/extensions/type",
         "in": ["CustomScript", "CustomScriptExtension"]}
      ]
    },
    "then": {"effect": "Deny"}
  }
}
EOF
az policy definition create \
  --name 'deny-custom-script-arc' \
  --display-name 'Block CustomScript extension on Arc servers' \
  --rules deny_custom_script.json \
  --subscription '<sub>'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-custom-script-arc' \
  --policy 'deny-custom-script-arc' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>'

# Pattern 2: AUDIT extension version (flag outdated extension versions)
cat << 'EOF' > audit_ama_version.json
{
  "mode": "All",
  "policyRule": {
    "if": {
      "allOf": [
        {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/extensions"},
        {"field": "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/extensions/type",
         "equals": "AzureMonitorLinuxAgent"},
        {"field": "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/extensions/typeHandlerVersion",
         "less": "1.29"}
      ]
    },
    "then": {"effect": "Audit"}
  }
}
EOF
az policy definition create \
  --name 'audit-ama-version-arc' \
  --display-name 'Audit outdated AMA version on Arc servers (< 1.29)' \
  --rules audit_ama_version.json \
  --subscription '<sub>'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'audit-ama-version-arc' \
  --policy 'audit-ama-version-arc' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>'

# Pattern 3: Check extension compliance via Resource Graph
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines/extensions'
   | project
       Machine=split(id, '/extensions/')[0],
       ExtName=name,
       Version=tostring(properties.typeHandlerVersion),
       Status=tostring(properties.provisioningState),
       AutoUpgrade=tobool(properties.enableAutomaticUpgrade)" \
  --query 'data' -o table

# Pattern 4: Enable automatic upgrade for all extensions on Arc servers
# automaticUpgrade=true lets Azure update extensions within the minor version band
az connectedmachine extension update \
  --name 'AzureMonitorLinuxAgent' \
  --machine-name '<hostname>' \
  --resource-group 'arc-servers-rg' \
  --enable-auto-upgrade true

Arc-enabled servers support VM extensions (imperative, installed via gc_extension service) and Guest Configuration policies (declarative, evaluated via gc_config service). Extensions install agents — AMA, MDE, CustomScript, Key Vault, OpenSSH, Windows Admin Center. GC policies audit OS settings using built-in (CIS, NIST, Windows/Linux Security Baseline) or custom DSC/InSpec packages. DINE-effect policies bridge the two by automatically deploying extensions to non-compliant servers — always grant the assignment MSI Contributor (for extensions) or Guest Configuration Resource Contributor (for GC packages). For AMA, two DINE policies are needed: install AMA + associate DCR. Custom GC packages require the GuestConfiguration PowerShell module, DSC configuration, and versioned package upload to Azure Storage.
